md: Some quantities have only a *size* — temperature, distance, mass. We call those **scalars**. Other quantities have a *size and a direction* — velocity, force, displacement. Those are **vectors**, and they're what physics, engineering, computer graphics, and pretty much any 2D/3D math runs on.
### Notation
A 2D vector with horizontal component $a$ and vertical component $b$ is written:
**Component form**: $\langle a, b \rangle$.
**Bracket-free**: $a\mathbf{i} + b\mathbf{j}$, where $\mathbf{i}$ and $\mathbf{j}$ are unit vectors along the x- and y-axes.
**Bold or arrow**: $\mathbf{v}$ or $\vec{v}$ to distinguish from scalars.
### Magnitude and direction
The **magnitude** $|\mathbf{v}|$ is the vector's length — the Pythagorean theorem on the components:
The **direction** is the angle $\theta$ from the positive x-axis (counterclockwise):

blocks.title: Try the widget
blocks.body: Drag the sliders for $u_x, u_y, v_x, v_y$ — watch the magnitudes and angles update. Set $\mathbf{u} = \langle 3, 4 \rangle$ and notice $|\mathbf{u}| = 5$ — the classic 3-4-5 triangle showing up again.

md: ### Adding vectors
**Component-wise.** Add the matching components:

blocks.tex: \langle a, b \rangle + \langle c, d \rangle = \langle a + c, b + d \rangle

md: **Geometrically — head to tail.** Draw $\mathbf{u}$ from the origin. Draw $\mathbf{v}$ from the head (tip) of $\mathbf{u}$. The sum $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}$ is the vector from the origin to the tip of the *translated* $\mathbf{v}$. The widget above shows this — the dashed copy of $\mathbf{v}$ starts where $\mathbf{u}$ ends.
### Scalar multiplication
Multiplying a vector by a scalar (just a number) scales its length and possibly flips its direction:
$|k\mathbf{v}| = |k| \cdot |\mathbf{v}|$. If $k > 0$, direction is unchanged. If $k < 0$, direction reverses.

blocks.title: Unit vectors
blocks.body: A **unit vector** has magnitude $1$. Any non-zero vector $\mathbf{v}$ can be turned into a unit vector pointing the same direction by dividing by its magnitude: $\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{v}}{|\mathbf{v}|}$. Useful when you want just the *direction* without caring about the magnitude.

md: ### Worked example
$\mathbf{u} = \langle 2, -1 \rangle$, $\mathbf{v} = \langle -3, 5 \rangle$. Find $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}$, $|\mathbf{u}|$, and $3\mathbf{u}$.
$\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = \langle 2 + (-3), -1 + 5 \rangle = \langle -1, 4 \rangle$.
$|\mathbf{u}| = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$.
$3\mathbf{u} = \langle 6, -3 \rangle$. Magnitude: $|3\mathbf{u}| = \sqrt{45} = 3\sqrt{5}$ — exactly $3$ times the original magnitude, as expected.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Vector = magnitude + direction. Components $\langle a, b \rangle$. Magnitude $\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$. Add component-wise (or head-to-tail geometrically). Multiply by a scalar to scale (and possibly flip). Divide by magnitude to get a unit vector.
